name: bogling warrior
shortDescription: A squat bogling warrior with marsh-stained gear, quick hands, and the stubborn courage of something used to fighting from mud. Collectors of bestiary curiosities value the figure, while frontier folk mostly value knowing what one looks like before it starts shouting from reeds.
flavorText: “Small warriors are still warriors when the swamp is on their side.”

name: bogling wizard
shortDescription: A bogling wizard marked by damp charms, crooked focus tools, and the unsettling confidence of a creature that learned magic where books rot quickly. Its value lies in odd study, swamp superstition, and the sort of power that smells faintly of moss and bad choices.
flavorText: “Never trust a spell that begins with bubbling mud.”

name: oxen
shortDescription: A strong working oxen team fit for plows, wagons, mills, and the heavy labor that turns fields into coin. They are slow stock, but slow is not a flaw when a merchant needs patience, muscle, and a living answer to bad roads.
flavorText: “Speed boasts. Oxen deliver.”

name: donkey
shortDescription: A sturdy donkey with sharp ears, a wary eye, and more usefulness than dignity usually receives credit for. It carries sacks, climbs poor paths, survives lean fodder, and refuses nonsense with the quiet authority of an animal that knows its own worth.
flavorText: “A donkey says no in the oldest language of trade.”

name: mule
shortDescription: A dependable mule bred for strength, stubborn judgment, and road work where horses become expensive complaints. Caravans prize mules because they carry more than pride, eat less than nobles expect, and remember bad footing better than many drivers.
flavorText: “Half horse, half donkey, all opinion.”

name: black horse
shortDescription: A black horse with a glossy coat and a dramatic presence that makes even ordinary riders look important for a few moments. Traders watch its legs, teeth, and temperament carefully, because a beautiful horse can still be a costly argument in a stable.
flavorText: “Black coats hide mud poorly, but ambition perfectly.”

name: brown horse
shortDescription: A brown horse of practical color and broad market appeal, suited to road travel, farm work, light carting, and riders who need reliability more than poetry. It is the sort of animal that sells without spectacle because everyone knows what work it can do.
flavorText: “The common horse is common because it keeps proving useful.”

name: golden horse
shortDescription: A golden-coated horse bright enough to draw attention across a fairground, prized by nobles, heralds, and wealthy riders with a taste for visible fortune. Its color raises the price before anyone checks the gait, which is exactly why careful buyers do both.
flavorText: “A golden horse makes every road look like a procession.”

name: gray horse
shortDescription: A gray horse with a calm, weathered look and the useful ability to seem wiser than its owner. It suits messengers, travelers, and households that want a dependable mount whose color wears dust and age with a certain quiet grace.
flavorText: “Gray horses look as if they have already heard the bad news.”

name: white horse
shortDescription: A white horse with ceremonial beauty, hard upkeep, and a habit of turning every puddle into an accounting problem. Princes, temples, and parade captains love it, while stablehands know its true cost is counted in brushes as much as coin.
flavorText: “White horses are never sold alone; they come with cleaning debts.”

name: abstract painting of despair
shortDescription: An abstract painting built from heavy shapes, sinking tones, and brushwork that makes the room feel colder without explaining why. It appeals to collectors, philosophers, and nobles who insist sadness becomes culture when framed well enough.
flavorText: “Some paintings decorate a wall. This one lowers the ceiling.”

name: abstract painting of animosity
shortDescription: A tense abstract painting full of clashing lines and hostile color, the sort of work that makes dinner guests argue before anyone mentions politics. Dealers call it bold, rivals call it unpleasant, and wealthy buyers call it whatever raises the value.
flavorText: “It has no subject, yet somehow takes offense.”

name: abstract painting of melancholy
shortDescription: A quiet abstract painting of softened edges, muted color, and a sadness too refined to become despair. It sells well to poetry houses, old estates, and anyone who wants a room to look thoughtful without admitting it feels lonely.
flavorText: “Melancholy is sorrow that learned to sit politely.”

name: masterpiece painting of Pearlgate beach
shortDescription: A masterfully painted view of Pearlgate beach, catching pale sand, bright surf, and the shining commerce of a coast where beauty and profit share the tide. Collectors prize the detail, but homesick sailors tend to stare the longest.
flavorText: “The sea looks calmest when someone else had to paint the wind.”

name: portrait of mother
shortDescription: A tender portrait of a mother, rendered with careful eyes and the small domestic details that keep memory from fading into mere shape. It is personal art, difficult to price fairly, because buyers often pay for the feeling rather than the paint.
flavorText: “Some faces are bought only because they were missed.”

name: amateur painting of snow
shortDescription: An amateur snow painting with earnest white fields, uncertain shadows, and enough charm to forgive its clumsy perspective. It suits cheap inns, student rooms, and buyers who prefer honest effort over costly mastery when brightening a wall.
flavorText: “Bad snow is still quieter than a bare wall.”

name: portrait of young woman
shortDescription: A portrait of a young woman painted with soft features, careful light, and enough restraint to leave her story politely unfinished. Dealers sell the craftsmanship, but viewers often invent the subject's history before asking the artist's name.
flavorText: “A good portrait answers less than it asks.”

name: fabled painting of Crownfall king
shortDescription: A fabled painting of the Crownfall king, rich with royal symbols, stern posture, and the dangerous polish of sanctioned memory. Courts, loyalists, historians, and collectors all want it for different reasons, which is how rare art becomes political currency.
flavorText: “Kings die once in flesh and endlessly in paint.”

name: masterpiece painting of Wolfhook Bay
shortDescription: A masterpiece view of Wolfhook Bay, showing cold water, hard docks, and ships that seem ready to creak out of the frame. Coastal merchants prize it as art, while captains quietly judge whether the painter understood weather at all.
flavorText: “A bay is a harbor until the wind changes its mind.”

name: beautiful painting of plant
shortDescription: A beautiful plant painting with careful leaves, graceful stem work, and colors chosen to make botany feel almost noble. Herbalists appreciate the accuracy, but most buyers simply enjoy owning something green that will not wilt, shed, or demand water.
flavorText: “Painted plants survive every negligent gardener.”

name: masterpiece painting of western landscape
shortDescription: A sweeping western landscape painted with masterful distance, warm light, and the wide silence of lands that make travelers feel both brave and small. It is prized in eastern parlors, where wilderness looks safest behind varnish.
flavorText: “The west is most comfortable when framed and hung indoors.”

name: masterpiece painting of Greenhollow forest
shortDescription: A masterwork depiction of Greenhollow forest, layered with deep greens, filtered light, and the suggestion of paths that may not forgive trespassers. Nobles admire the craft; woodsfolk admire that the painter left some shadows unexplained.
flavorText: “Greenhollow never looks empty. That is the point.”

name: fabled painting of Pearlgate champion
shortDescription: A fabled painting of the Pearlgate champion, posed in triumphant light with the sea-bright pride of a city that remembers its heroes loudly. It carries the value of art, fame, and local vanity in one carefully protected frame.
flavorText: “A champion wins once. A painting lets the city keep winning.”

name: masterpiece painting of Appleford landscape
shortDescription: A masterpiece landscape of Appleford, full of orchard rows, soft hills, and the prosperous calm of land that knows its harvest will be admired. It is a favorite among wealthy households that want country virtue without country mud.
flavorText: “Appleford looks sweetest when someone else owns the ladder.”

name: portrait of father
shortDescription: A portrait of a father painted with steady posture, worn expression, and the kind of small severity that families remember long after arguments fade. Its market value depends on craft, but its private value often depends on forgiveness.
flavorText: “Paint can soften a face that life left stern.”

name: portrait of old man
shortDescription: A portrait of an old man, lined with careful age, watchful eyes, and the patience of someone who has outlived too many explanations. Collectors like the character; apprentices study the wrinkles; families sometimes wonder who he disappointed.
flavorText: “Every wrinkle is a road the painter had to follow.”

name: fabled painting of Redvale queen
shortDescription: A fabled painting of the Redvale queen, composed with regal color, controlled grace, and the unmistakable message that mercy and command can wear the same jewels. It is prized by courts, rivals, and anyone trading in symbols of power.
flavorText: “Queens rarely need crowns in portraits. The room already knows.”

name: fabled painting of Embercourt prince
shortDescription: A fabled portrait of the Embercourt prince, glowing with courtly heat, youthful ambition, and the dangerous certainty of someone painted before history had finished judging him. Collectors pay for the name, the rarity, and the rumors around both.
flavorText: “Princes are easiest to admire before they inherit consequences.”

name: beautiful painting of boqueue
shortDescription: A beautiful painting of boqueue, with gathered blossoms arranged in a bright composition that forgives the spelling faster than appraisers do. It brings color to parlors, shops, and guest rooms, offering floral cheer without petals on the floor.
flavorText: “A painted boqueue never wilts, though critics may.”

name: fabled painting of Border March champion
shortDescription: A fabled painting of the Border March champion, all hard lines, weathered courage, and frontier pride made suitable for a noble wall. March families see a hero; collectors see rarity; soldiers see whether the boots look believable.
flavorText: “Border heroes are painted with more dust than gold.”

name: masterpiece painting of Embercourt harbor at night
shortDescription: A masterpiece night view of Embercourt harbor, lit by lanterns, reflected fire, and the dark water where fortunes arrive quietly. It is valuable for craft alone, but smugglers and captains admire how honestly it paints the usefulness of shadow.
flavorText: “At night, every harbor becomes a secret with ropes.”

name: masterpiece painting of quaint supper
shortDescription: A masterful supper scene full of warm bowls, low light, and ordinary comfort made precious by the painter's hand. It sells especially well to rich buyers who enjoy remembering simple meals from a safe distance.
flavorText: “Nothing flatters wealth like a beautiful picture of modest food.”

name: amateur painting of desert
shortDescription: An amateur desert painting with earnest dunes, harsh sun, and uncertain proportions that somehow still captures heat well enough to make viewers thirsty. It is cheap art, but caravans sometimes buy such pieces as jokes, warnings, or memories.
flavorText: “Even badly painted sand gets everywhere.”

name: beautiful painting of flowers
shortDescription: A beautiful flower painting with generous color, soft detail, and enough brightness to rescue gloomy rooms from themselves. It is popular with inns, households, and gift buyers who want beauty that survives travel better than actual flowers.
flavorText: “Painted flowers are the florist's most durable competition.”

name: fabled painting of western princess
shortDescription: A fabled painting of a western princess, dressed in regional finery and a gaze carefully balanced between softness and command. Its value travels far beyond the west, carried by romance, politics, and the market's weakness for royal mystery.
flavorText: “A princess in paint can rule rooms she never entered.”

name: drums
shortDescription: A set of drums for festivals, signals, taverns, marching feet, and any gathering that needs courage made louder. They are bulky, hard to ignore, and easy to sell in towns where silence has grown too expensive or too suspicious.
flavorText: “A drum is a crowd before the crowd arrives.”

name: flute
shortDescription: A slim flute with a clear voice, easy to carry and difficult to master without annoying everyone nearby first. Travelers, shepherds, court players, and lonely watchmen all value it because a small instrument can fill a large evening.
flavorText: “The flute proves that air can be persuaded.”

name: harp
shortDescription: A graceful harp with tuned strings, polished frame, and the delicate authority of music meant to quiet rooms rather than rouse them. It is costly to transport safely, but nobles and temples pay well for sound that seems refined before the first note.
flavorText: “A harp makes even silence sit straighter.”

name: lute
shortDescription: A wooden lute suited to tavern songs, court verses, traveling minstrels, and love declarations of uneven quality. Its value depends on tone, frets, and whether the last owner treated it as an instrument or a shield against thrown fruit.
flavorText: “A lute can earn supper, trouble, or both.”

name: trumpet
shortDescription: A bright trumpet built to carry sound over crowds, roads, courtyards, and battlefield confusion. Heralds love it for authority, performers for attention, and innkeepers only when the player has already paid for the room.
flavorText: “Subtlety has never owned a trumpet.”

name: violin
shortDescription: A polished violin with a singing voice suited to courts, wakes, dances, and skilled hands that can make joy and grief share the same string. Buyers inspect the wood carefully, because a fine violin remembers every careless repair.
flavorText: “Four strings are enough to empty a purse or a heart.”

name: black paint
shortDescription: A pot of black paint used for signs, ships, trim, shadow work, and anything that needs to look serious quickly. Artists call it essential, builders call it practical, and thieves prefer not to explain why they bought so much of it.
flavorText: “Black paint is where color goes to become certain.”

name: blue paint
shortDescription: A pot of blue paint valued for shutters, signs, heraldic work, shrine details, and brightening objects that would otherwise look merely useful. Good blue is harder to mix than buyers expect, which lets painters argue price with confidence.
flavorText: “Blue sells best in towns that miss the sea.”

name: brown paint
shortDescription: A practical brown paint for furniture repair, crates, beams, wheels, doors, and all the little lies used to make old wood look intentional. It is not glamorous, but tradesmen buy it steadily because usefulness has many shades of brown.
flavorText: “Brown paint is honesty with a repair bill.”

name: crimson paint
shortDescription: A rich crimson paint for banners, sigils, fine trim, theatrical masks, and dramatic rooms owned by people allergic to subtlety. Its color commands attention, which makes it valuable to heralds, artists, and merchants selling anything meant to look urgent.
flavorText: “Crimson does not decorate. It announces.”

name: green paint
shortDescription: A pot of green paint used for garden gates, shop signs, stage scenery, festival masks, and devotional work tied to growth or luck. The right shade feels fresh; the wrong shade makes everything look seasick, so careful buyers open the lid.
flavorText: “Green paint promises spring even in a warehouse.”

name: lime paint
shortDescription: A sharp lime paint with a bright, almost impudent color, useful for festival props, marked crates, children's toys, and signs that need to be seen from a foolish distance. It sells poorly to the tasteful and wonderfully to the loud.
flavorText: “Lime is green after too much applause.”

name: orange paint
shortDescription: A warm orange paint suited to lantern signs, harvest decorations, stage pieces, and wagons meant to be spotted in crowds. It adds cheer quickly, though painters warn that too much of it can make a room feel like a shouting pumpkin.
flavorText: “Orange paint turns caution into decoration.”

name: pink paint
shortDescription: A pink paint favored for toys, festival booths, delicate trim, sweet shops, and clients who want softness made visible. It is easy to mock until it sells three times faster than respectable gray in the right market.
flavorText: “Pink has survived every serious person's objection.”

name: red paint
shortDescription: A dependable red paint for doors, signs, wagon wheels, warning marks, festival props, and anything that needs the eye before the mind. It is common, useful, and always in demand wherever merchants must compete with noise.
flavorText: “Red paint is the shortest route between looking and noticing.”
